**The INSANE Engineering of Formation Spaceflight**

**INTRO**  
Formation-flying – a not so widely discussed topic when people talk about spaceflight, yet it will serve a huge purpose in the future of it.

**PROBA-3**

Meet PROBA-3, one of the most fascinating satellites currently in orbit around our blue home. Or should I say two satellites… In fact, the reason PROBA 3 is so fascinating is that it uses highly precise formation flying to achieve its previously thought impossible goal of photographing the corona of the sun in great detail, at any time\!

Previously we could only image the corona on the very special occasion of a solar eclipse, or using a coronagraph. This is a circle that is held in front of a camera lens to block out the intensive light from the sun, and reveal the corona. However, a disadvantage of this is that image quality is compromised heavily. The only solution to that is to make it bigger…  
PROBA-3 – a program carried out by over 40 member countries of ESA, that aimed to do exactly that, make it bigger. To be precise, 144 meters long\!

Now the hard part begins, how exactly do you make such a big structure… in SPACE\! You certainly can’t make a booms that long to hold the circle in place. The International Space Station is about 109m long, so that would mean making a single structure of that is even longer that the ISS. That would be a huge difficulty to both carry into orbit and assemble since the Spaceshuttle program that built the ISS is retired for a long time. Next to that, those booms would also compromise the view of the camera.

The solution… Make 2 entirely separate spacecraft that stay in a perfect line with respect to the sun. Fly them 144 meter apart would make you a 144 meter long coronagraph. The coronagraph spacecraft carries the camera and the occulter spacecraft had a huge circular disk used to block out coming from the sun. 

![][image1]

**How do you fly in formation**

Alignment of these two spacecraft is critical for mission success and is a matter of millimeter precision. To achieve this goal, engineers designed a system that uses a combination of small white reflectors on the occulter disk and a light mounted on the coronagraph spacecraft. These bright reflectors can be easily picked up by a camera, and used to determine any compensation needed to maintain formation.

This formation is so delicate that even the orbit of the mission had to be carefully chosen to avoid any disturbances. Disturbances of what, you might ask? Well, after a heavy amount of research it turns out that individual mountain ranges can mess with the positioning of both spacecraft relative to each other\!  
The chosen orbit is a highly elliptical one with an apogee of about 60.000 km and a perigee of 600km. The mission is designed in such a way that the vehicles only enter formation mode when they are far enough from earths surface, to when these imperfections become negligible. From the moment formation is entered, there is approximately a six hour window for continuous imagery, a normal solar eclipse only lasts a fraction of that.

![][image2]
